# RepoBrain — Demo Script (3-minute walkthrough)

*Generated by launch-package skill | 2026-04-14*
*Target duration: 2:30–3:30 | Word count target: 375–525 words*
*Narration pace: 150 wpm | This script: ~450 words (~3:00)*

---

## HOOK (0:00–0:20)

How long did it take you to understand the last codebase you joined?

Most engineers I know say a week, minimum. You trace the same call chain three times because nothing is documented where you'd look. You ask a colleague "where does X happen?" and they have to check. You make a change, something breaks that you didn't know was connected.

The codebase isn't wrong. It's just opaque.

Let me show you how RepoBrain changes that.

---

## CONTEXT SETUP (0:20–0:40)

Right now, your options for understanding unfamiliar code are: read it directly, search for keywords, or ask someone who knows. All three are slow. None of them tells you what will break if you change something.

Here's RepoBrain. You're looking at a workspace connected to a TypeScript repo — 140,000 lines, indexed 8 minutes ago. The indexing pipeline ran symbol extraction, embedded every code chunk, and built a full relation graph. That's done. Now we can ask questions.

---

## CORE FEATURE (0:40–1:40)

Let's start with a question you'd normally spend 45 minutes on manually.

"Where does request authentication happen?"

Watch this. RepoBrain runs three retrieval strategies in parallel. Semantic search finds code that conceptually matches the question — even if the word "authentication" doesn't appear. Lexical search matches keywords across the full text. Structural search follows symbol relations — function calls, imports, type uses — to find what connects to what.

The results merge and rank. The LLM builds its answer from what's actually in the repo.

Here's the answer. Notice what's different: those are citations. auth.ts line 47. middleware.ts line 23. session.ts line 89.

Click auth.ts:47. The code viewer opens — you're at the exact line. The middleware check. The session validation. You can read it, you can quote it, you can open a PR from here.

That's not a feature. That's the reason you can trust the answer. The AI cited what retrieval returned. It can't tell you about code that doesn't exist in your repo.

---

## WOW MOMENT (1:40–2:20)

Now I want to show you something you can't do with Copilot Chat or ChatGPT on your code.

Type this into the search bar: "find error handlers without retry logic."

This is structural natural language search. RepoBrain classifies the intent — you want catch blocks, filtered by whether retry logic appears within their scope. It's not grepping for the words "error" and "retry." It knows what the code does, not just what it says.

Three results. Click the first — it's a catch block in the payment service. Look at the surrounding function: no retry call anywhere in scope. That's accurate. That's the structural retrieval layer working in real time.

And this is why three-strategy retrieval matters. Copilot Chat doesn't know your repo's structure. RepoBrain does.

Navigate any codebase — from first question to confident answer — without guessing.

Index your first repo free. The link is in the description. Three minutes to connect, eight minutes to index a medium-sized repo. You'll have your first cited answer before your next meeting.
